title: A Procedure for Revising Data-Based Priors in a Group
abstract: A group of individuals is interested in predicting the outcome of a current problem. Each individual has access to private data that are used to form a prior probability over possible outcomes. While individuals may be reluctant or unable to disclose their private data, they are willing to publish their priors. We characterize a procedure for revising the individualsвҖҷ prior probabilities based on the published priors of others and discuss its implications regarding the formation of a common prior in the group, both in the short and long run.

article.1. Introduction: Group forecastingвҖ”the Delphi method, the nominal group method, and the dialectical method1вҖ”is a common organizational practice used to better meet workplace goals. In these interactive forecasting techniques, group members are required to act in concert in order to improve predictions. Although some of these techniques are more methodical than others, all enjoin group members to form individual forecasts, which can then be revised in response to feedback from other group members. In this paper, we suggest a possible account of how individuals revise their probabilities in social contexts where they are exposed to the probability estimates of others. To illustrate, consider a pharmacovigilance (drug safety) committee that convenes in order to evaluate the safety and effectiveness of a certain vaccine for a specific risk group. For example, they may be trying to assess the efficacy of the influenza vaccine in the elderly or the safety of the measles vaccine in children infected with HIV. The committee members are all specialists belonging to different medical institutions and HMOs. Each member holds a prior belief regarding the safety and efficacy of the vaccine based on statistical data of patients that belong to her or his own institution. The main objective of their collaboration is to improve their evaluations by each member contributing his or her expertise and experience. For concreteness, suppose that memberA, who initially evaluated the probability that the vaccine is safe to be 0.7, now learns that memberBhas evaluated that probability at 0.95. ShouldAadjust his or her initial evaluation upon learning the evaluation ofB? On the one hand,BвҖҷs evaluation indicates that his or her experience in similar past cases contains a lower proportion of adverse reactions than does that ofA, which could makeAwant to increase his or her initial evaluation. On the other hand, the safety of the vaccine can vary, depending on the characteristics of the person being vaccinated, andAandBmay perceive different past cases as similar to the risk group under consideration. For example,Bmay include adults with HIV in his or her evaluation of the measles vaccine for children, whereasAmay view them as irrelevant.Amay, therefore, choose to disregard the evaluation ofB, not knowing on which past casesBhas based his or her evaluation. To overcome this difficulty, we assume that individuals discuss the reasoning behind their probabilistic evaluations, thereby identifying the common ground underlying their evaluations. Subsequently, the evaluations that are shared are based on past cases with agreed-upon relevance only. This is in line with the perspective of the Global Vaccine Safety Initiative (GVSI) that meaningful communication and exchange of information within the system of international pharmacovigilance must rely on common terminology. Consequently, the Global Advisory Committee on Vaccine Safety developed standardized case definitions for specific adverse events following immunization (seeBonhoeffer et al. 2002). To formalize the evaluation process described in the example, our model builds onBillot et al. (2005)(BGSS), which provides a framework for the formation of prior probabilities over possible outcomes in a current case based on past observations. An observation is composed of characteristics of a past case and the outcome that was realized in that case. For example, a vaccination record could be characterized by age, risk group, medical condition, and so on, with the outcome being the reaction to the vaccine. According to BGSS, the evaluator has a subjective similarity function over past cases that reflects the degree to which he or she expects outcomes in the current case to resemble the outcomes of past cases. The probability of an event is a weighted average of the frequency of occurrence of that event in the database, wherein past observations that are considered to be more similar to the current case are assigned higher weights. In our model, we consider a group of individuals conforming to the BGSS model, each forming a probability over outcomes based on private data, using subjective similarity weights. Importantly, we suppose that individuals cannot reveal their private data. For instance, the data may be confidential, as is the case of clinical or legal data where doctors and lawyers are forbidden to release personal information without the consent of their patients or clients. Similarly, companies that collect data that can be used to identify individuals must have privacy policies in place that state that the companies are legally obliged to protect personal information, and thus, they too cannot freely share data. For example, under the provisions of the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), organizations are responsible for securing the privacy of personal data on European Union citizens. Alternatively, an individualвҖҷs private database need not be an actual database but only a metaphor for personal experience, and thus it may not be shareable at all. In this paper, we examine how individuals can benefit from sharing their probability assessments even if they cannot share their raw data. Within our model, there are two sources of heterogeneity in the formation of individual priors; one is the diversity of private databases, and the other is disagreement on how relevant past cases are to the current case. Because the two sources are intertwined, individuals cannot fully trust their peersвҖҷ private probabilities. To better understand the kind of evaluations that could be useful to group members, we present inSection 3.1a behavioral condition termed Peer Pareto, which identifies past cases with an agreed-upon degree of relevance to the current problem. We then assume that the probability assessments published within the group rely only on observations with agreed-upon degrees of relevance, which we refer to asagreed-upon observations. Thus, these published probabilities, in contrast to private ones, are meaningful to the group members. Having been informed of the published priors of their peers, members of the group revise their initial, private probabilities. This is in line with the experimental findings ofSniezek and Henry (1990), wherein subjects were asked to provide initial estimates for prices of vehicles, as well as revised estimates that were obtained after group discussions. The revised estimates were shown to be less heterogeneous and closer to the group estimates compared with individual initial estimates. Such a procedure can be described by the linear opinion rule, as referred to byStone (1961), whereby the revised opinion is a weighted average of the individualsвҖҷ opinions and is justified on the basis of the Pareto principle. However, asGenest and Zidek (1986)rightfully state, the choice of the weights lacks any logical explanation and is, therefore, arbitrary. By contrast, in our paper (inSection 3.2) we present an axiomatic characterization of the weights that can serve as a normative foundation for the linear opinion rule that we propose. The resulting revised priors of individuals are a weighted average of their private prior and the published priors of their peers, where the weights depend on the data underlying these priors. The resulting revised priors of individuals are a weighted average of their private prior and the published priors of their peers. An important difference, however, from the standard linear opinion rule, is that the weights within the revised priors depend on the quality of the data underlying these priors. Nevertheless, as explained next, no disclosure of the data themselves is required. In principle, we allow the weights assigned to an individualвҖҷs private prior and those of the other group membersвҖҷ published priors to be any function of that individualвҖҷs private database and the agreed-upon observations within the othersвҖҷ databases. However, weights that satisfy the four simple assumptions listed below will, in fact, depend only on one number provided by each group member, indicating the quality of the individualвҖҷs agreed-upon data. We refer to these numbers asquality scores. The revised prior based on these characterized weights equals the prior that the individual would have formed had group members pooled their agreed-upon databases. Hence, although individuals publish only their agreed-upon priors and the quality scores of the agreed-upon parts of their databases, it is as though they had shared their agreed-upon observations. There is no need to actually disclose data. The main assumptions that characterize the weights assigned to the private and published priors are impartiality and update consistency. In impartiality, it is supposed that the weights do not depend on the realized outcomes within a database but only on the characteristics of the observations composing it, eliminating any a priori bias toward any outcome. Update consistency addresses situations where members update priors based on information regarding the occurrence of an event. The axiom states that it is inconsequential whether this update takes place before or after revising the initial priors upon learning the published priors of others. InSection 4, we describe a process of adjustment of probabilities over time. In each period, new individuals join the group. Observations that are agreed upon by all group members, old and new, are identified. Subsequently, new members publish their probabilities that are based upon the agreed-upon observations in their private databases and their corresponding quality scores. In response, all group members update their probabilities, following the aforementioned process. Although the similarity values of each individual remain constant, as more individuals join the group, higher weight is assigned to a diminishing set of agreed-upon observations. As a result, the revised probabilities of individuals gradually converge to a common prior. Notably, in the special case where there exists sufficient heterogeneity in similarity values (formally explained inSection 4), the common prior will equal the empirical frequency of outcomes in observations with characteristics that are identical to those of the current case, a frequency that is arguably the best predictor of the true distribution over outcomes. This process resembles the approach of kernel estimation, wherein less distant observations are assigned higher weight as the size of the database grows. The papers that are most related to our work areDe Groot (1974)andLehrer (1976), which proposed a method that allowed individuals in a fixed group to revise their subjective probabilities after being informed of the subjective probabilities of the other group members. In those models, a given individualвҖҷs revised probability was a convex combination of the probabilities of group members from the previous period, where this process was repeated indefinitely.2In these papers, the main question was when group members could reach a consensus regarding the probabilities at issue.Chatterjee (1975),Chatterjee and Seneta (1977), andWagner (1978)extended this model by allowing weights to change over time. The main difference between these models and our own is that in our model the revision of probabilities takes place only if new information is introduced. With a fixed number of individuals with fixed databases, our revision process terminates after just one period, and the revised probabilities of individuals after one period need not be the same. Only if individuals share the same similarity function will their probabilities coincide. InGolbub and Jackson (2010), individuals in a social network, who have independent private signals regarding the true probability, follow the De Groot-Lehrer model with respect to their immediate neighbors.Golub and Jackson (2010)found conditions regarding the structure of the network that enabled individuals to learn the true probability when the number of individuals in society grew to infinity. The influence of all individuals must approach zero for individual вҖңmistakesвҖқ to cancel out.Cerreia-Vioglio et al. (2023)characterized a more general sequential updating process, providing conditions for convergence of evaluations to the true value of the parameter. In our sequential process, as more individuals join the group, eventually, only observations that are identical to the current problem will be used to evaluate the probability (with a sufficient amount of heterogeneity in the population). With a large enough sample, the resulting probability will be close to the true probability. Mailath and Samuelson (2020)addressed a related question of how agents infer information relevant to their own models from the probabilities of other agents with different models. In that paper, states of nature were multidimensional, and each agent considered only a subset of those dimensions as being relevant to the prediction, corresponding to that agentвҖҷs model. Agents observed private information regarding a subset of dimensions that obtained and updated their priors according to their models. If they could infer from the other agentsвҖҷ beliefs information relevant to their own model, they revised their probabilities accordingly. Updating terminates when beliefs are no longer revised. The setting ofMailath and Samuelson (2020)is completely different than ours, however; they had analog results to the results mentioned above.Mailath and Samuelson (2020)showed that if all agents have the same model (the analog being a similarity function in our paper), they will end up with the same beliefs; however, when agents have different models, their beliefs may differ. Moreover, they showed that under certain conditions, the average belief will be close to the true probability.

article.3. Sharing Probabilistic Beliefs: Clearly, individuals would benefit from gaining access to the databases of other individuals, for then each individual would form a probability based on the combined databases. However, as noted in the introduction, sharing private databases is not always possible. In those situations, we ask whether a group of individuals might benefit from the exchange of priors without revealing their raw data and, if so, to what extent. In this section, we assume that individuals share probability assessments that are derived exclusively from their agreed-upon observations, which are first identified. After learning their peersвҖҷ probabilities, individuals respond by revising their own probabilities. We examine the characteristics of a revision process that for each individual is posited to be a linear combination of the individualвҖҷs private probability and the published probabilities of his or her peers. 3.1. Peer Pareto and Agreed-Upon Observations In this subsection, we identify observations that serve as a foundation for probabilities shared among peers. For this purpose, we present a condition on attribute vectors that guarantees the meaningfulness of probabilities that are based on observations with these attributes. This condition calls for every individual to envision what he or she would do if everybody in the group had access to identical data composed of such observations and whether they would agree with a unanimous likelihood ranking of their peers in such a situation. LetЛңрқ”ёвҠҶрқ”ёAЛңвҠҶAbe a subset of attributes. We say that Peer Pareto holds onЛңрқ”ёAЛңif for every group memberрқ‘–вҲҲрқҗјiвҲҲI,any databaseрқҗ·вҲҲвҲӘрқ‘ҹвүҘ1(Лңрқ”ёГ—О©)рқ‘ҹDвҲҲвҲӘrвүҘ1(AЛңГ—О©)r,and any pair of events E and F, Peer Pareto is trivially satisfied for every singleton set{рқ‘Һ}вҠӮрқ”ё{a}вҠӮAforathat is relevant for all individuals (hence, there are multiple setsЛңрқ”ёAЛңon which Peer Pareto is satisfied). However, individuals might wish to include more than one attribute vector inЛңрқ”ёAЛң. The process of identifying which attribute vectors can be included inЛңрқ”ёAЛңis viewed as a thought experiment rather than an exhaustive comparison across all databases and individuals. It prompts individuals to contemplate the circumstances under which they would rely on their peersвҖҷ unanimous probabilistic judgments based on whether their peersвҖҷ analysis of the data aligns with their personal perspective. The setЛңрқ”ёAЛңof agreed-upon observations arises from the intersection of these individual reflections. Individuals will readily adopt the unanimous probability rankings of their peers if they accept the logic underlying their peersвҖҷ formation of their probabilities. This is established in the next theorem that shows that sustainment of Peer Pareto onЛңрқ”ёAЛңis equivalent to the coincidence of individual similarities on the attribute vectors inЛңрқ”ёAЛң. LetЛңрқ”ёвҠҶрқ”ёAЛңвҠҶAbe a subset of attribute vectors such thatрқ‘ҺвҲ—вҲҲЛңрқ”ёaвҲ—вҲҲAЛң, and then Peer Pareto holds onЛңрқ”ёAЛңif and only if the similaritiesрқ‘ рқ‘–,рқ‘–вҲҲрқҗјsi,iвҲҲI,when restricted toЛңрқ”ёAЛң,are identical. The proof of the theorem appears inSection A.1. It uses a separation argument to show that if the individualsвҖҷ similarities are not the same, it is possible to construct a database with attributes inЛңрқ”ёAЛңfor which Peer Pareto is violated. The idea underpinning the theorem is that when individuals disagree on the degree of relevance of past observations to the problem at hand, that is, when they employ different similarity functions, they cannot fully trust each otherвҖҷs priors. However, when restricted to databases on which these individuals share the same view on how to analyze the data, namely, when restricted to databases on which their similarity functions coincide, group members know that had they been given the same data as others they would have formed the exact same priors. In that case, priors are indicative of the data that were used to generate them. An interesting point is that in contrast toHarsanyi (1968), who associated differences in subjective probabilities with differences in information alone, we allow differences in subjective probabilities to also result from differences in interpretation of the same information. Hence, differences in subjective probabilities may arise whenever individuals use different similarity functions for the formation of probabilities, even if their priors are based on the same data. An implication ofTheorem 1is that individuals can identify the setЛңрқ”ёAЛңby comparing their similarity values and incorporating intoЛңрқ”ёAЛңonly attribute vectors whose similarity values are the same across all individuals. This conclusion follows from the premise inTheorem 1thatрқ‘Һ*вҲҲЛңрқ”ёa*вҲҲAЛң. On a technical level, this condition ensures thatЛңрқ”ёAЛңcomprises relevant attribute vectors and, coupled with the normalization inAssumption 1(ii), that similarity values are identical across individuals. More importantly, we consider the inclusion ofрқ‘Һ*a*inЛңрқ”ёAЛңa reasonable prerequisite becauseЛңрқ”ёAЛңseeks to uncover shared foundations on which individuals base their probability assessments. This holds especially true whenЛңрқ”ёAЛңis determined prior to individuals observing their data and becauseрқ‘Һ*a*is generally recognized by all as the most relevant attribute vector for the problem at hand. However, there may be cases where this requirement is restrictive. For example, if it is known that there are other sets on which Peer Pareto is satisfied that excludeрқ‘Һ*a*but yield significantly higher similarity scores for all individuals compared with those that containрқ‘Һ*a*(seeSection 5.1for a discussion of alternative methods for selecting agreed-upon sets of attribute vectors). Hereafter, we setЛңрқ”ёAЛңto be the maximal subset of attributes on which Peer Pareto holds and that containsрқ‘ҺвҲ—aвҲ—. This subset is nonempty becauseрқ‘ҺвҲ—aвҲ—belongs to it, and it is unique because it is the maximal set on which individualsвҖҷ similarities coincide. Observations with attribute valuesathat belong toЛңрқ”ёAЛңare referred to as agreed-upon observations. The first axiom, called impartiality, maintains that the weights assigned to the individualsвҖҷ prior probabilities do not depend on the realized outcomes within databases but only on the attributes of the observations composing them. Whereas the weights can depend on the size of databases or the quality of the data underlying the individualsвҖҷ prior probabilities, they cannot rely on the probability assessments of the individuals, thereby asserting that there is no a priori bias toward any of the outcomes. For example, consider a politician who is trying to assess his or her chances of winning the next elections. He or she asks several pollsters to conduct opinion polls on his or her behalf, targeting the relevant population. Impartiality would fail if the politician would choose to disregard the results of polls that predict that he or she has a low probability of winning. It would be sustained if the weights of the different polls were assigned before the results are learned. Impartiality complements the data-based, similarity weighted average method employed by the individuals in the formation of their prior probabilities. According to this method, the weight assigned to an observation within a database depends on the similarity between the attributes of this observation and those of the current problem and not on the realized outcome, just as compelled by impartiality. Note that whereas the weights are independent of the realized outcomes, the prior probabilities are not. Impartiality. The reasoning underpinning symmetry depends on the assumption that only agreed-upon observations are used to compute published priors so that two individuals with the same agreed-upon data will publish the same priors. From a practical point of view, the advantage of update consistency is that there is no need to ask for peersвҖҷ probabilistic assessments every time new information arrives regarding the occurrence of an event. Instead, individuals need only require the published priors of their peers once and then update their own revised priors using BayesвҖҷ rule whenever new information becomes available. Update consistency is reminiscent ofMandaskyвҖҷs (1964)External Bayesianism axiom, which is an expression of group rationality. Regarding the effect that information has upon updating, External Bayesianism states that if probabilities are to be revised based on some information, it should make no difference whether they are updated before or after aggregating the group membersвҖҷ priors. It is well known that unless it is a dictatorship, the linear opinion pool with constant weights (representing the individualsвҖҷ expertise) violates External Bayesianism (Genest et al. 1986).6The problem lies in the fact that the weights do not change as new information regarding individualsвҖҷ expertise accumulates. In particular, it seems reasonable that upon learning that a certain event occurred, the weights of those who predicted this event with a higher probability would increase. This shortcoming, however, does not apply to our model, although we impose update consistency, because the weights assigned to individual probabilities herein depend on the databases of the individuals. The revised priors at timet>sof these two individuals can be expressed as a weighted average of two probabilities, one on which they may differ and the other on which they agree: The probabilitiesрқ‘қрқ‘—вҲҳрқ‘Ўв„“=рқ‘ Лңрқҗ·в„“pвҲҳв„“=stDЛңв„“jandрқ‘қрқ‘ҳвҲҳрқ‘Ўв„“=рқ‘ Лңрқҗ·в„“pвҲҳв„“=stDЛңв„“kare equal because they are based on observations that have been agreed upon by all individuals. Moreover, weightsрқӣјрқ‘—рқ‘ЎОұtjandрқӣјрқ‘ҳрқ‘ЎОұtkthat multiply the components on whichjandkmay disagree decrease over time, and therefore, their revised priorsМ…рқ‘қрқ‘—рқ‘ҹ,рқ‘ЎpВҜr,tjandМ…рқ‘қрқ‘ҳрқ‘ ,рқ‘ЎpВҜs,tkapproach each other. The reason is that the revised priors rely on a growing number of agreed-upon observations, which override the number of observations on which individuals disagree. This follows from our assumption that in each periodtnew members add new relevant agreed-upon data, or at least this must hold true infinitely often. Otherwise, there would be only a finite amount of new information that is gathered, and from a certain point in time onward the priors of group members would not change. Overtime setЛңрқ”ёрқ‘ЎAЛңtmay shrink because it contains attribute vectors that should be agreed upon by an increasing group of individuals, yet it never becomes empty because it must containрқ‘ҺвҲ—aвҲ—. The sequence of setsЛңрқ”ё1,Лңрқ”ё2,вҖҰAЛң1,AЛң2,вҖҰis therefore decreasing, with a nonempty limiting set. Moreover, because there is a finite number of attribute vectors, there must be a periodt0such that from that time on all setsЛңрқ”ёрқ‘ЎAЛңtare the same. Therefore, the corresponding revised priors converge to a common prior that is the similarity weighted empirical frequency of outcomes within the limiting set of agreed-upon observations. Whether this limiting set is equal to{рқ‘ҺвҲ—}{aвҲ—}or contains additional attribute vectors depends upon the distribution in the population of similarity values. If people prefer joining groups that share many of their own views, namely, the groupвҖҷs similarity is the same as theirs for many attribute vectors in addition toрқ‘ҺвҲ—aвҲ—, thenЛңрқ”ёрқ‘ЎAЛңtwill not converge to{рқ‘ҺвҲ—}{aвҲ—}. However, if for each attribute vectorрқ‘Һвү рқ‘ҺвҲ—aвү aвҲ—there is a positive probability of disagreement in the population over its degree of relevance, then the limiting set will equal{рқ‘ҺвҲ—}{aвҲ—}. With that kind of disagreement in the population the revised priors of all individuals will tend toward a common prior, which is the empirical frequency of outcomes within observations whose attributes are identical to those of the current case. It stands to reason that these observations best represent the true probability of occurrences of outcomes in the current problem, so the common prior is the true probability. The process we describe is in line with the method of kernel estimation, a nonparametric technique that is used to estimate the conditional expectation of a dependent variableygiven the values of the independent variables (seeAkaike 1954,Rosenblatt 1956,Parzen 1962,Silverman 1986, and others). Roughly speaking, the estimation of a dependent variableyis a weighted average of all values ofyin the data set, where the kernel determines how much weight is to be placed on each observation based on the independent variables. The evaluation process that appears inTheorem 2is in fact a form of kernel estimation, where the probability of some event of interest is the dependent variable and the attributes are the independent variables. Typically, in kernel estimation in small samples, positive weight is placed on more distant observations in order to reduce the variance of the estimator. This, in turn, yields a biased estimator. However, as the sample size grows, the weight put on distant observations decreases, leading to a consistent estimator. This is precisely what happens in the process described above where the sequence of setsЛңрқ”ёрқ‘ЎAЛңtshrinks to{рқ‘ҺвҲ—}{aвҲ—}. At first, individual priors may be biased because they rely upon observations whose attribute values can differ considerably from those of the current case. But at that stage, putting all the weight on observations with attributesрқ‘ҺвҲ—aвҲ—would lead to a noisy estimation because there is only a small number of such observations. As time progresses, weight shifts to the most relevant observations, that is, those with attributesрқ‘ҺвҲ—aвҲ—, which enables individuals to learn the true distribution of outcomes. The link to kernel estimation has already been pointed out byGilboa et al. (2006). However, in that paper, the kernel represented by the similarity function remained fixed regardless of the size of the database. In contrast, in the current paper, the similarity of a single individual is also fixed, but the kernel that emerges within the group in the proposed process does change. These changes are caused by the fact that larger groups of individuals tend to agree on a smaller set of similarity values, mimicking the adjustment of the weights in kernel estimation methods.

In such cases, a different approach to selectingЛңрқ”ёAЛңmight be more suitable. These normalized values that are comprehensible to all individuals can be utilized by individualiby multiplying the reported similarity scores byрқ‘ рқ‘–вҒЎ(рқ‘ҺвҖІ)siвҒЎ(aвҖІ)and then carrying out the revision rule as described inTheorem 2with the adjusted scores. It is important to note that our main findings are not sensitive to the choice of^рқ”ёA^. In our model, each individual shares only one prior probability that is based on a single set of (relatively) agreed-upon attribute vectors. However, this revision process can be extended to encompass several statistics, which can help mitigate the loss of information stemming from the limitation of sharing only agreed-upon information. In particular, an alternative approach is to partition the non-agreed-upon data into finely divided cells, where each cell contains only observations with relatively agreed-upon similarities. Individuals can then publish a table of statistics, comprising their respective priors and normalized similarity scores for each cell. These published statistics can be simultaneously employed to revise the prior probabilities according to the adjusted process. Such a partition exists because the finest partition, wherein each cell contains only a single attribute vector, meets this requirement.8 It is important to understand that the privacy of records could be compromised when using very fine partitions, where the number of observations in certain cells could be low. Recognizing this limitation, the OECD presented a threshold rule stating that вҖңa cell in a table of frequencies is defined to be sensitive if the number of respondents is less than some specified number. [вҖҰ] When thresholds are not respected, an agency may restructure tables and combine categories or use cell suppression [вҖҰ] to satisfy the rule.вҖқ9Our model suggests that only cells with relatively agreed-upon observations should be aggregated, thus preserving the meaningfulness of the information. 5.2. Group Priors In many group decisions, group members are expected to integrate their beliefs into a single prior that serves as a basis for decision making. This raises the question of which prior group members will adopt. One principle that might guide the decision is a Pareto condition imposed on the private priors of individuals to the effect that whenever there is a unanimous agreement on the ranking of two prospects, the group prior will coincide with that ranking. However, the consensus is not necessarily genuine when group members use different similarities to form beliefs. In particular, two members may happen to agree on the ranking of prospects given their own private data but would reach the opposite conclusion had they access to their peerвҖҷs private data. The same holds true for revised priors because they are also based on private data. This may be viewed as a data-based version of вҖңspurious unanimityвҖқ presented byMongin (1997), where it is argued that the Pareto principle is much less compelling when it entails agreement based upon controversial reasoning. This problem can be overcome by imposing the Pareto principle on group membersвҖҷ published priors, because those are formed using agreed-upon data. Just as inTheorem 2, this Pareto condition together with the axioms that appear inSection 3.2provide a characterization of the weights that depend on the similarity scores of group membersвҖҷ agreed-upon databases.10The group prior that emerges is the similarity-weighted average over the aggregated agreed-upon data of all group members. Note, however, that data that are not agreed upon are ignored, and therefore, this process is more acceptable when there is ample agreed-upon data. 5.3. Relaxing Symmetry Between Individuals InTheorem 2, the weights assigned to individual prior probabilities are determined solely by the total similarity of each individualвҖҷs respective database. This outcome is a consequence of the symmetry axiom, which states that prior probabilities should receive equal weight when they are based on the same data. However, certain experimental studies suggest that individuals tend to favor their own opinions over those of others (Yaniv and Kleinberger 2000). An Alternative Interpretation of Peer Pareto In this paper, we employ the interpretation that Peer Pareto is an instrument for identifying those attribute vectorsЛңрқ”ёвҠҶрқ”ёAЛңвҠҶAfor which fundamental agreement on their relevance to the question at hand obtains. However, Peer Pareto when imposed on the entire setрқ”ёAmay be viewed as a normative principle of open-mindedness. It states that for every group memberрқ‘–вҲҲрқҗјiвҲҲI, every databaseD, and any pair of eventsEandF, Under this interpretation, individualрқ‘–вҲҲрқҗјiвҲҲI, knowing that all of his or her peers, having been given the same data asi, believe that one event is more likely to occur than another, will choose to adopt the belief of the group rather than holding an independent view. This requirement may be considered weak because to satisfy it individuals need only agree on the probability ranking of events when this ranking is consensual, whereas the mildest disagreement within the group of peers removes all restrictions imposed on that ranking. Conversely, the requirement that Peer Pareto must apply to every database may be thought of as a strong condition. Indeed, a consequence ofTheorem 1is that the special case where Peer Pareto is satisfied onрқ”ёAentails that the similarity functions of all individuals must coincide and, therefore, also their probability assessments whenever these are based on the same data. 5.5. Weak Peer Pareto The version of Peer Pareto that we employ in the paper is strict. If at least one of an individualвҖҷs peers judges an event to be strictly more probable than another and all of the other peers weakly agree, then that individualвҖҷs ranking of the likelihood of the two events will also be strict. Alternatively, weak Peer Pareto is satisfied if for every group memberрқ‘–вҲҲрқҗјiвҲҲI, every databaseD, and any eventEandF, Theorem 1establishes that when the strict version of Peer Pareto is satisfied onрқ”ёA, a common similarity for all individuals ensues. By contrast, in the weak version of Peer Pareto, similarities do not necessarily coincide, yet every similarity that is an extreme point ofрқ‘җрқ‘ңрқ‘ӣрқ‘ЈвҒЎ{рқ‘ рқ‘–|рқ‘–вҲҲрқҗј}convвҒЎ{si|iвҲҲI}will have to be held by at least two individuals. The weaker form of Peer Pareto does not allow us to identify observations that are interpreted in the same manner by all individuals. This makes the revision of priors inSection 3.2problematic. The same holds true for the weak version of Peer Pareto that is satisfied only on a subset ofрқ”ёAas inDefinition 1. 5.6. Applicability of the Linear Opinion Rule We make no assumptions regarding the interdependencies of the individualsвҖҷ databases. Yet if, for example, some observations appear in more than one database, then applying our revision process to those databases would lead to a bias of the revised priors because of double counting. This is akin to the persuasion bias ofDeMarzo et al. (2003), in which individuals fail to account for repetition in the information they have received.11A similar difficulty could arise if observations that appear in different databases are correlated. Thus, although our theoretical result holds true regardless of the interdependencies of individualsвҖҷ databases, the proposed revision process best applies to circumstances wherein the observations that appear in any two databases are disjointed and uncorrelated, for instance, when the data are cross-sectional as in the medical example above. To identify problems where our revision process is applicable, we formulate a Pareto-type condition. The acceptance of this condition is equivalent to the individualsвҖҷ revised prior being a linear pool, which is a convex combination of their private prior and the published priors of their peers. The condition states that if the private prior of individuali, as well as the published priors ofiвҖҷs peers, all indicate that the expected value of prospectXis greater than that of prospectY, then the revised prior of individuali,М…рқ‘қрқ‘–pВҜi, should arrive at that same conclusion. An evaluator may be reluctant to satisfy this condition if the databases largely overlap or if the observations across databases are highly correlated. In this case, it may be that the individualsвҖҷ unanimous agreement is based on the same observations that happen to appear in all of the individualsвҖҷ databases, whereas the reverse assessment is supported by observations that differ across databases and that together are substantial enough to reverse the unanimous ranking. On the other hand,Condition 1is suitable for problems wherein the relevance of observations of an individual is independent of those of the other individuals, for example, when priors rely on separate and independent sources of information.
